coredata.dc:title: Monitoring food spoilage using smart plant-based packaging materials: Methylcellulose/soy protein films loaded with betacyanin and carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ebrahimi, Alireza
Kiani-Salmi, Narges
Tavassoli, Milad
McClements, David Julian
Ehsani, Ali
Khezerlou, Arezou
Sani, Mahmood Alizadeh
coredata.dc:description: In this study, novel plant-based smart packaging materials were developed by loading betacyanin (MJ-Bs) and carbon dots (MJ-CDs) isolated from Mirabilis jalapa petals into biofilms fabricated from soy protein isolate (SPI) and methyl cellulose (MC). The MJ-Bs and MJ-CDs were used as natural color indicators and antimicrobials. Based on scanning electron microscopy analysis, these films had smooth surfaces and uniform structures. Incorporating the MJ-CDs and MJBs into the biofilms increased their UV-blocking properties (from 96.8 to 99.9 %), wettability (from 49.1 to 76.3 °), and tensile strength (from 29.3 to 36.3 MPa), and reduced their water vapor permeability (from 2.91 to 2.13 × 10–11 g. m/m2. s. Pa). The color of the MJ-CD/MJB-loaded biofilms exhibited strong sensitivity to changes in pH (2 to 12), as well as to changes in ammonia vapor levels. The potential application of the biofilms was assessed by measuring their ability to monitor and preserve shrimp freshness during storage at 25 °C. The color of the biofilms shifted from pink (fresh) to brownish yellow (spoiled) during storage, indicating their efficacy for this purpose. These findings highlight the potential of these novel biofilms for quality evaluation in protein-rich fresh food products, offering significant implications for food safety, shelf-life extension, and sustainability in the packaging industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biodegradable polymer
pH sensing labels
Active/Intelligent packaging
Protein-rich food

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of betacyanin and synthesis of CDs
Colorimetric and transmittance properties of biofilms
Packaging test of SPI-MC-based intelligent biofilms
Color sensitivity tests of the biofilms
Comparison of efficacy of biofilms with different compositions
Ethical statement - studies in humans and animals

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Photoluminescence spectra of MJ-CD suspensions after excitation using different wavelengths (300–440 nm); (b) UV–visible spectra of MJ-CD suspensions; (c) Dynamic light scattering analysis of particle size distribution of MJ-CDs; (d) Antibacterial activity of MJ-CDs against E. coli and S. aureus.
a) SEM images (surface); (b) FTIR spectra, and (c) XRD patterns of SPI-MC, SPI-MC/MJ-CDs, and SPI-MC/MJ-CDs/MJBs biofilms.
a) Digital pictures and (b) light transmittance spectra of SPI-MC, SPI-MC/MJ-CDs, and SPI-MC/MJ-CDs/MJBs biofilms.
a) Tensile strength and elongation at break; (b) water vapor permeability; and (c) water contact angle of SPI-MC based biofilms with different compositions.

Changes in the visual appearance of (a) aqueous solutions containing MJ-CDs and MJBs and (b) SPI-MC biofilms containing MJ-CDs and MJBs when exposed to different pH (2–12) values, (c) tristimulus color coordinates of SPI-MC biofilms containing MJ-CDs and MJBs before and after NH3 exposure.

a) Photographs of SPI-MC/MJ-CDs/MJAs biofilm for shrimp freshness monitoring on different storage times; (b) Color difference values (ΔE) of SPI-MC/MJ-CDs/MJAs biofilm of shrimp samples at the corresponding storage times; and (c) The change in pH value of shrimp samples at 0 – 48 h of storage.
L*, a*, and b* values and light transmittance of the SPI-MC based smart biofilms.
Comparison of prepared SPI-MC/MJ-CDs/MJAs biofilm with other reported CD-based films.

list-item.para: Color changes from red to colorless
Color changes from red to colorless/yellow
Color changes from pink/reddish to dark yellow/brownish
Color changes from red to dark yellowish

para.bold: Color changes from pink to brownish yellow

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: GEL: Gelatin, VCCDs: Vaccinium corymbosum-carbon dots, CNFs: Cellulose nanofiber, BOA: Brassica oleracea anthocyanin, CAR: Carrageenan, SPA: Sweet potato anthocyanin, RP: Rose petals, FG: Fish gelatin, BA: Butterfly pea flower anthocyanin, TS: Tensile strength, EAB: Elongation at break.

head.ce:title: Monitoring food spoilage using smart plant-based packaging materials: Methylcellulose/soy protein films loaded with betacyanin and carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, novel plant-based smart packaging materials were developed by loading betacyanin (MJ-Bs) and carbon dots (MJ-CDs) isolated from Mirabilis jalapa petals into biofilms fabricated from soy protein isolate (SPI) and methyl cellulose (MC). The MJ-Bs and MJ-CDs were used as natural color indicators and antimicrobials. Based on scanning electron microscopy analysis, these films had smooth surfaces and uniform structures. Incorporating the MJ-CDs and MJBs into the biofilms increased their UV-blocking properties (from 96.8 to 99.9 %), wettability (from 49.1 to 76.3 °), and tensile strength (from 29.3 to 36.3 MPa), and reduced their water vapor permeability (from 2.91 to 2.13 × 10–11 g. m/m2. s. Pa). The color of the MJ-CD/MJB-loaded biofilms exhibited strong sensitivity to changes in pH (2 to 12), as well as to changes in ammonia vapor levels. The potential application of the biofilms was assessed by measuring their ability to monitor and preserve shrimp freshness during storage at 25 °C. The color of the biofilms shifted from pink (fresh) to brownish yellow (spoiled) during storage, indicating their efficacy for this purpose. These findings highlight the potential of these novel biofilms for quality evaluation in protein-rich fresh food products, offering significant implications for food safety, shelf-life extension, and sustainability in the packaging industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The widespread use of plastics derived from fossil fuels as packaging materials poses significant environmental threats due to their appreciable contribution to greenhouse gas emissions and their persistence within ecosystems after disposal [1,2]. It has been predicted that plastic production will increase to 1.1 billion tons by 2050 [3,4], which is likely to exacerbate these issues. To address these challenges, it is important to reduce plastic production, increase recycling rates, and develop innovative strategies for plastic waste management [5,6]. Additionally, there is a pressing need to develop more sustainable alternatives to conventional petroleum-based plastics. For instance, the synthetic polymers currently used in the food packaging industry could be replaced by biodegradable polymers that are more sustainable and environmentally friendly [7]. Biodegradable packaging reduces pollution by naturally breaking down in the environment, thereby reducing landfill waste and plastic accumulation. Moreover, it supports sustainability by using renewable resources, thereby promoting a circular economy and reducing the reliance on fossil fuels [8]. Consequently, there is a need to develop effective and economically viable biodegradable packaging materials from renewable resources, such as plant-derived components [9].

Soy proteins can be used to formulate biodegradable food packaging materials due to their abundance, affordability, biodegradability, and good film-forming properties [10,11]. Previous studies have found that soy protein-based packaging materials have good oxygen and oil barrier properties, but they have poor water barrier, moisture resistance, and mechanical properties [12]. Consequently, functional additives are required to overcome these limitations. The mechanical and barrier properties of soy protein-based films can be improved by blending them with other biopolymers. For instance, methyl cellulose is a linear polysaccharide derived from cellulose that can be used for this purpose [13]. Methyl cellulose is comprised of chains of glucose molecules joined by β-(1→4) glycosidic linkages, where some of the hydroxyl groups (OH) are replaced by methyl groups (CH3), contributing to its unique physicochemical and functional properties [14]. Methyl cellulose has good biodegradability, as demonstrated by its ability to degrade under environmental conditions [15]. Moreover, methyl cellulose-based films with high clarity and good mechanical properties can be economically produced in high volumes, making them suitable for commercial applications [16,17].

Functional additives can also be used to introduce new functionalities into biodegradable packaging materials. For instance, they can be used to create smart or active packaging materials designed to monitor and/or extend the shelf life of foods [18,19]. Betacyanins are a group of natural pigments found in various plants, including fruits, vegetables, and flowers, which are water-soluble and safe [20]. They possess several functional properties that make them suitable for application in biodegradable films, including strong antioxidant activity, antimicrobial effects, and the ability to change color in response to alterations in environmental pH [21]. Betacyanins can be isolated from Mirabilis jalapa (M. jalapa), which is a decorative plant found in tropical and subtropical regions that belongs to the genus Mirabilis and the family Nyctaginaceae [22]. M. jalapa is traditionally used in medicine to treat gastrointestinal disorders, muscle pain, inflammation, and skin conditions, as well as to promote wound healing and act as a diuretic. In addition, it is commonly used in the food industry in jellies and cakes as a natural coloring agent [23–25].

Another source of plant-derived additives with potential application in biodegradable food packaging materials are carbon dots (CDs) [26,27]. These novel nanomaterials are spherical, carbon-based quantum dots with dimensions below 10 nm, which gives them distinctive physicochemical and functional characteristics [28]. For instance, CDs have been shown to have good affordability, biocompatibility, water solubility, antioxidant activity, antimicrobial activity, UV protection properties, and strong luminescence [29,30], which may be useful for enhancing the functional performance of biodegradable food packaging materials. Previous studies have shown that CDs can be produced from a variety of sustainable plant-based sources [31].

In this study, we examined the potential of using Mirabilis jalapa betacyanins (MJBs) and Mirabilis jalapa carbon dots (MJ-CDs) as additives in biodegradable films. The MJ-CDs were derived from Mirabilis jalapa petals, a waste product generated during the extraction of betacyanin from Mirabilis jalapa. The polymer matrix of the biodegradable films was constructed from plant-derived components: soy protein isolate (SPI) and methyl cellulose (MC). The impact of MJ-CD and MJB integration on the mechanical, UV shielding, water vapor permeability, surface hydrophilicity, and pH-sensitive color-changing characteristics of the SPI-MC biofilms was investigated. The potential application of these biofilms was then assessed by examining their potential for tracking the freshness of shrimp during storage at 25 °C. The knowledge gained from this study may facilitate the rational design of biodegradable food packaging materials with enhanced performance.

Soy protein isolate (SPI) with a high protein content (90 %) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Methyl cellulose (MC) was sourced from Henkel (Düsseldorf, Germany). Food-borne pathogenic bacteria, Escherichia coli (PTCC:1860) and S. aureus, (PTCC:1431), were obtained from the microbiology laboratory of the Infectious Research Center (Tabriz, Iran). 2, 2-diphenylpicrylhydrazyl (DPPH) was provided by the Sigma Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Medium cultures of bacteria such as Plate Count Agar (PCA) and Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA) were obtained from the Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany). All other chemicals and solvents, including hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetone, and acetic acid, were purchased from the Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of betacyanin and synthesis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The MJBs were extracted from the M. jalapa petals using the following procedure. Initially, 5 g of powdered petals and 100 mL of a mixture of distilled water and ethanol (80:20 v/v) were mixed and then stirred at 25 °C for 1 h. The resulting mixture was then filtered through Whatman filter paper (No. 1), and the filtrate was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the supernatant was concentrated using a rotary evaporator at 50 °C. The resulting extract was then stored in a dark glass container at 4 °C for future analysis.

The MJ-CDs were synthesized from M. jalapa petals using a hydrothermal method. Initially, 1 g of biowaste residue of M. jalapa petals was dispersed in 50 ml of deionized water. The resulting mixture was then placed in a stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the reaction product was centrifuged at a speed of 9000 rpm for 40 min. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter (Sartorius-Stedim Biotech) to eliminate impurities. The resulting filtrate was then stored in a refrigerator prior to use.

The particle size of the MJ-CDs was determined using a photon correlation spectroscopy-based particle size analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd, UK). The fluorescence responses of the MJ-CDs were characterized using a spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer LAS, Germany). The functional groups in the MCCD samples were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Model 4300, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) over the wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm⁻¹.

The antimicrobial activity of the MJ-CDs was assessed using a disk diffusion method. Initially, a 0.5 McFarland concentration of the desired bacterial suspension was prepared and then evenly spread onto the surface of an agar plate using a pre-prepared sterile swab. Next, 8 mm diameter wells were created in the agar, into which 80 μl of MJ-CD dispersions (4 mg/mL) were transferred. Finally, the cultured plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the diameter of each zone of inhibition was measured.

The SPI-MC-based biofilms were fabricated using a solution casting method described previously [32]. Initially, 1 g of powdered SPI was dispersed in 100 mL of distilled water and then heated to 60 °C while stirring continuously for 2 h. This solution was then adjusted to pH 7.0 by adding 0.1 M NaOH solution, and the resulting mixture was allowed to cool to 25 °C and then equilibrated for 24 h at this temperature with continuous stirring using a magnetic stirrer. Independently, 1 g of powdered MC was dispersed in 100 mL of purified water and then stirred at 25 °C for 24 h until completely dissolved. The SPI solution (50 mL) was then mixed with the MC solution (50 mL), and 20 mL of 200 mM citric acid was added as a crosslinking agent. Glycerol (35 wt % based on polymer weight) as a film plasticizer and 3 v/v % of MJ-CD dispersion were then added into this mixture. The resulting solution was then stirred continuously at 4500–5000 rpm for 2 h at 55 ± 2 °C. Next, 5 v/v % of MJBs were gradually added while stirring for 15 min. The biofilm solution was subsequently transferred to a Petri dish and allowed to dry for 48 h at 25 °C. Following the drying process, the biofilms were kept at 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity for a minimum of two days before testing.

A scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, TESCAN Mira3, Czech Republic) was used to characterize the surface morphology of the films. First, the films were coated with a 10 nm thick gold layer (DST1, Nanostructure Coating Company, Tehran, Iran) in an argon-enriched environment. The gold-coated films were then imaged using a scanning electron microscopy instrument operated at 10 kV.

Information about the composition and molecular interactions within the biofilms was obtained by measuring their infrared spectra using a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker Tensor 27 spectrophotometer, Bruker, Germany). Spectra were acquired over the wavenumber range from 4000 to 400 cm-1.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was used to provide insights into the crystalline or amorphous nature of the biofilms. Initially, the biofilms were cut into 1 cm × 1 cm squares and then their XRD patterns were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance, Karlsruhe, Germany) over the diffraction angle (2θ) range from 10 to 60° at a temperature of 25 °C.

The thickness of all biofilms was measured using a digital micrometer (Fowler, USA). For mechanical analysis, the biofilms were cut into dumbbell forms (0.5 × 8 cm) and then positioned between the 2 arms of a tensile testing instrument (Instron 5566, USA). The films were then stretched at a rate of 50 mm/min and the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) were determined from the resulting stress-strain curves.
The water vapor permeability (WVP) of the biofilms was assessed using the ASTM E96–05 (ASTM, 2005) method described previously [33].

The water contact angle (WCA) of the biofilms was quantified by analyzing digital images of a small aliquot of water placed on their surfaces using a digital camera (Microsoft, LifeCam, H5D-00,013, zoom 24). In summary, 10 µl of deionized water were first placed injected onto the surface of a biofilm and then a digital image was taken after a stable contact angle between the water and film was attained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and transmittance properties of biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: The pH responsiveness and chromatic alteration characteristics of the MJ extracts and SPI-MC/MJ-CDs/MJB biofilms were assessed in various buffer solutions with pH levels of 2, 4, 6, 7, 8, 10, and 12. The ultraviolet and visible (200–600 nm) light transmittance spectra of the biofilm specimens were also recorded using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The ammonia gas reactivity of the indicator biofilms was evaluated by suspending them above an ammonia (0.8 M) solution for 30 min, and then characterizing their chromatic responses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Packaging test of SPI-MC-based intelligent biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Shrimp were purchased from a local supermarket (Tabriz, Iran). The shrimp were then placed in a petri dish and a biofilm was positioned above it, without any direct contact between the shrimp and film. The petri dishes were then stored at 25 °C for 48 h, and any chromatic changes in the biofilms were documented using digital photography.

All analyses were conducted in triplicate, and the results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical data analysis was performed using IBM SPSS Statistics 22 (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY), employing one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Additionally, Duncan's multiple range test was used to assess the differences among groups, with a significance level set at P < 0.05.

Initially, the optical properties of the MJ-CDs were evaluated using photoluminescence spectroscopy by measuring the intensity of the fluorescence emission when the samples were illuminated at different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence emission moved to higher wavelengths as the excitation wavelength rose (Fig 1
a). The MJ-CDs had a peak fluorescence emission at 509 nm when an excitation wavelength of 400 nm was used (Fig. 1 b). When an excitation wavelength of 365 nm was utilized, the MJ-CDs solution exhibited a bright blue hue, which can be attributed to electron transitions within π → π∗ bonds of CC and n → π∗ bonds of CC [34]. In general, the rate of discoloration of MJ-CDs after exposure to UV irradiation depends on the carbon source and the manufacturing process used to create them [35]. The dynamic light scattering analysis showed that the MJ-CDs were roughly 1 nm in diameter (Fig. 1 c). These results showed that the MJ-CDs were relatively small fluorescent particles, which may therefore have potential for application in biodegradable films as colorimetric sensors.

The antibacterial activity effects of the MJ-CDs against S. aureus and E. coli were evaluated using the disc diffusion method (Fig. 1 d). The diameters of the inhibition zones were ∼ 14 mm and 7 mm for S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. These results suggest that the antimicrobial effects of the MJ-CDs were greater against S. aureus (Gram-positive bacterium) than against E. coli (Gram-negative bacterium). Typically, the absence of an outer membrane makes Gram-positive bacteria more vulnerable to the damaging effects of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can penetrate the cell wall and destroy it [36]. The underlying mechanisms for the antibacterial effects of MJ-CDs against different bacteria are currently unknown. However, previous studies suggest that the small size of MJ-CDs causes them to quickly penetrate into bacterial cells and destroy them by changing cell metabolism [37–39].

A variety of analytical methods were used to provide insights into the morphologies, interactions, and physical states of the different films. The surface morphology of the biofilms is shown in Fig. 2
a. The SEM images of the surfaces of the SPI-MC biofilms (no additives) showed that they were relatively smooth, but that they did contain some surface irregularities, which may have been biopolymer aggregates or other insoluble matter within the soy protein or methyl cellulose ingredients used to formulate them. When the MJ-CDs were added, the surface morphology of the SPI-MC biofilms was fairly similar, indicating that the presence of the small carbon dots did not cause a major alteration in the structural organization of the films. Interestingly, when both MJ-CDs and MJBs were added, the surface morphology of the biofilms were relatively smooth. Overall, these results show that the MJ-CDs and MJBs had good compatibility with the surrounding SPI-MC polymer matrix.

The FTIR spectra of the different biofilms were measured (Fig. 2 b). The absorption peaks in the MC-SPI, MC-SPI/MJ-CDs, and MC-SPI/MJ-CDs/MJBs biofilms were all observed near 3429 cm-1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the O − H groups [40]. The peak observed around 2927 cm-1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C − H groups [40], while the peaks observed around 1646 and 1461 cm-1 were attributed to the amide I and II groups, respectively [41]. The FTIR spectra of the MC-SPI/MJ-CDs/MJBs biofilms exhibited peaks that closely resembled those observed for the MC-SPI biofilms, exhibiting only slight changes in peak intensity. This similarity may be due to peak overlaps associated with the stretching vibrations of functional groups (such as HO, NH, CC, CH, and C-S) that were common for different components, as well as the relatively low concentrations of the MJ-CDs and MJBs present within the MC-SPI matrix [40,42].

Typically, increasing the crystallinity of a biodegradable biofilm increases its hardness and density [43]. X-ray diffraction analysis was therefore used to quantify the crystallinity of the different biofilms (Fig. 2 c). All the biofilms exhibited similar spectra, having a single broad peak around 2θ = 21° and no sharp peaks, suggesting that the films had a predominantly amorphous structure. No large changes were observed in the XRD patterns of the MC/SPI films after adding the MJ-CDs and MJBs, which suggests that they did not alter the amorphous structure of the films. Other researchers have also reported that adding anthocyanins and CDs to film matrices did not change the intensity or position of the XRD peaks, which was mainly attributed to a strong compatibility between the additives and surrounding matrix [41].

The visual appearance of the different biofilms is shown in Fig. 3 . The SPI-MC biofilms (no additives) were highly transparent and colorless, which suggests that any heterogeneities they contained were much smaller than the wavelength of visible light, and that the soy protein and methyl cellulose ingredients used to formulate them contained no chromophores. Upon the incorporation of MJ-CDs, the biofilms turned a light-yellow hue but remained transparent (Fig. 3 a). This color change was related to the brown-yellow color of the MJ-CDs solution [44]. The subsequent addition of MJBs resulted in a dramatic color shift to dark pink, which was mainly attributed to the characteristic cherry red color of the MJBs (Fig. 3 a).

The UV light blocking and visible light blocking properties of the films were assessed by using measuring their absorbance values at 280 nm and 700 nm, respectively (Table 1). The SPI-MC biofilms exhibited low transmittance at both wavelengths, i.e., 3.16 % and 37.04 % at 280 and 700 nm, respectively. The incorporation of the MJ-CDs led to a significant reduction in the transmittance at both wavelengths (p < 0.05), suggesting enhanced UV and visible light-blocking capabilities. Notably, the T280 value diminished more remarkably (92 % reduction) than the T700 value (22 % reduction). Incorporating both MJ-CDs and MJBs further decreased the UV and visible light-blocking properties of the films (p < 0.05). Indeed, these films blocked around 95 % of the UV light at 280 nm. These results show that the incorporation of both MJ-CDs and MJBs imparted good UV barrier abilities to the SPI-MC biofilms, which may be useful for creating packaging materials for food products that are liable to degradation when exposed to UV light. However, this addition of these additives also changed the visible appearance of the films, which may be undesirable for some applications.

The thickness of the SPI-MC biofilm was 0.059 mm and increased to 0.073 mm and 0.079 when MJ-CDs and MJ-CDs/MJBs were added, respectively. Thus, the addition of solid fillers (MJ-CDs/MJBs) to the films enhanced their thickness, which is consistent with previous studies [47]. This effect can mainly be attributed to the fact that there was more material remaining after the films were air dried.

The permeability of biofilms to water vapor is a critical factor for preserving the safety and quality of packaged foods throughout storage [36]. This parameter is influenced by filler type, concentration, and interactions with the polymer matrix. Water vapor transport in biofilms involves absorption, diffusion, and evaporation processes. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the WVP of the SPI-MC biofilms (2.91 × 10–11 g.m/m2.s.Pa) decreased after the addition of the MJ-CDs (2.57 × 10–11 g. m/m2.s.Pa), and then decreased further after the addition of the MJBs (2.13 ± 0.03 × 10–11 g.m/m2.s.Pa). These findings indicate that both the carbon dots and the betacyanin interacted with the film matrix, leading to the creation of compact micro-structural films and diminishing their affinity for water molecules. Furthermore, the molecular structure of these substances can enhance the cross-network effect in SPI-MC films, restricting the movement of water vapor molecules and lowering the WVP [48]. In the case of WVP it is reported to reduce with the dual-doped addition of CDs and rose petals anthocyanin into carrageenan which according to Wagh et al., (2024) [49]. The decline in quality of many foods during storage is due to moisture transfer between the food and its surroundings. Therefore, reducing the WVP of biofilms is often desirable when creating food packaging materials [50].

Water contact angle (WCA) experiments were used to determine the wettability and hydrophobicity of the biopolymer biofilms (Fig. 4 c). In general, biofilms with a contact angle below 90° are considered to be hydrophilic, whereas those with a contact angle above this value are considered to be hydrophobic. All of the biofilms prepared in this study were predominantly hydrophilic, but their polarity did depend significantly on their composition. The SPI-MC biofilms had a contact angle of 49.2 ± 2.0° Incorporating MJ-CDs into these films led to an appreciable increase in the WCA value (65 ± 2.0°), which indicated that the films became less hydrophilic. This effect can be attributed to the hydrophobic nature of the carbon dots. Incorporating both MJ-CDs and MJBs into the SPI-MC films increased the WCA value further (76.3 ± 1.6°), making them still less hydrophilic. This result suggests that the betacyanin molecules were also hydrophobic. In addition, the betacyanin molecules may have formed hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups on the proteins and/or polysaccharides in the films, thereby reducing the exposure of these hydrophilic groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color sensitivity tests of the biofilms
ce:section.ce:para:
The color of the MJ extract solutions and biofilms were found to be pH-dependent (Fig. 5
a and 5 b). The extract exhibited a brick-red color at pH 2–4, a cherry-red color at pH 5–6, a bright yellow color at pH 7–9, and a light-yellow color at pH 10–12. The reddish color observed in acidic and neutral pH conditions can be attributed to the presence of high concentrations of betanidin, isobetanidin, and isobetanin residues. The yellow-brown color observed at pH > 9 can be attributed to the hyperchromic and bathochromic effects of compounds such as cycloDOPA glucoside, betalamic acid, and neobetacyanins/neobetanin, which become more prevalent in alkaline conditions [51]. The color of the SPI-MC biofilms containing MJ-CDs and MJBs exhibited a similar pH dependence as the solutions (Fig. 5 b), highlighting their potential for creating smart biofilms that provide a visual indication of pH changes in their environment.

The decomposition of protein-rich food typically results in the production of volatile substances, such as sulfides, amines, and ammonia [52]. Among these, volatile nitrogen compounds, primarily ammonia, cause an elevation in the pH of foods, which can therefore be used as an indicator of their freshness [53]. The potential sensitivity of the biofilms to deterioration mechanisms that generate ammonia was therefore investigated. The colorimetric properties (L*, a*, and b*) of the biofilms were assessed before and after exposure to ammonia. As shown in Fig. 5 c, a significant color change was evident in the biofilms following a 30 min exposure to ammonia. Visually, the SPI-MC biofilms containing MJ-CDs and MJBs had a pinkish color before being exposed to NH3, but a yellowish color after. This color transition was also reflected in the changes to the tristimulus color coordinates of this films. After ammonia exposure, the L* value increased appreciably, the a* value went from highly positive (reddish) to slightly negative (greenish), and the b* value went from slightly positive (light yellow) to highly positive (intense yellow). These color changes can be attributed to the interaction of volatile NH3 with H2O within the biofilm, leading to the formation of NH3: H2O complexes. Subsequent hydrolysis of these complexes releases NH4 + and OH− ions, thereby causing an increase in pH. These color changes may therefore be suitable for monitoring the generation of volatile ammonia during the deterioration of fresh food products during storage.

The potential performance of the smart biofilms developed in this study for monitoring the freshness of shrimp during storage was evaluated by placing them within transparent boxes (Fig. 6
a). The biofilms changed in color during storage from a pinkish color at the beginning of storage to a brownish-yellow color after 48 h storage. The observed change in appearance was accompanied by an appreciable increase in the color difference values (ΔE) of the films (Fig. 6 b), which could be used as a method of assessing their quality attributes.

In tandem with the observed visual changes, the pH of the shrimp samples increased from around 6.3 to 7.6 during storage (Fig. 6 c). In general, the acceptable upper pH limit for shrimp and other protein-rich foods before they become unsuitable for human consumption is taken to be 7.0 [54]. Thus, our pH change data suggests that the shrimp samples began to become unacceptable after about 36 h of storage and were completely unsuitable for consumption after 48 h of storage. These results are in agreement with previous studies that have also reported a pH increase in shrimp during storage [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of efficacy of biofilms with different compositions
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, we compared the mechanical, barrier, surface, sensing, and preservative properties of the biofilms prepared in this study with those reported in previous studies (Table 2). The films developed in this study had considerably lower water vapor permeabilities and higher water contact angles than those prepared in other studies, which may have been due to the strong hydrophobic nature of the carbon dots and betacyanin, as well as their ability to increase the crosslinking between the biopolymer chains. However, the tensile strength and elongation at break values were consistent with those reported in previous studies, being lower than some and higher than others. The films developed in this study may therefore be more suitable for applications where tighter moisture control is required.

Betacyanin (MJB) and carbon dots (MJ-CDs) were extracted from bio-waste materials isolated from Mirabilis jalapa petals. A novel smart biofilm was produced using soy protein isolate (SPI) and methylcellulose (MC) as film forming-materials, and MJBs, and MJ-CDs as functional additives that could monitor and reduce the spoilage of shrimp due to their pH-dependent colorimetric properties and antimicrobial activity. The MJ-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence and good antibacterial properties against both E. coli and S. aureus. The addition of MJBs and MJ-CDs to the SPI-MC biofilms enhanced their UV-blocking, water vapor permeability, wettability, and mechanical properties. These biofilms also exhibited a noticeable color shift when exposed to different pH values or ammonia gas, which could be used as a means of detecting the deterioration of protein-rich foods during storage. The biofilms could visually monitor the deterioration of shrimp during storage at 25 °C. As the shrimp spoiled, the biofilm's color changed from pinkish to brownish yellow, which was indicative of a pH increase. These findings indicate that natural pigments and carbon dots derived from food processing waste can be used as functional additives for improving the performance of biodegradable packaging materials, thereby contributing to a more circular economy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethical statement - studies in humans and animals
ce:section.ce:para: This study does not include any human or animal studies.

Alireza Ebrahimi: . Narges Kiani-Salmi: . Milad Tavassoli: . David Julian McClements: . Ali Ehsani: . Arezou Khezerlou: . Mahmood Alizadeh Sani: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was approved & supported by Student Research Committee, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran (Project No. 72682).
